metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the canonical script location in the plan (e.g., `infra/gerrit/materialize-opcert-secrets.sh`, consistent with the existing `materialize-deploy-key.sh` / `materialize-g2p-config.sh` precedent) so the executor has a single decided path.
findings.finding: The plan leaves the materialize script's destination directory unresolved: 'under `infra/gerrit/` or `infra/scripts/`' is an explicit either/or choice deferred to the executor, not a stated default. An agent must pick a location to create the file, and downstream references (compose volume mounts, CI paths) must be consistent.
findings.location: What section — compose/nginx bullet: 'a materialize script … under `infra/gerrit/` or `infra/scripts/`'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'under `infra/gerrit/` or `infra/scripts/`' with 'at `infra/gerrit/materialize-opcert-secrets.sh`' (matching the existing pattern).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the proving approach for the materialize-script rendering assertion in AC3 — e.g., 'tested via a bats-core unit test that stubs `aws ssm get-parameter` to return a fixed value and asserts the rendered nginx-conf include contains that value' — so an agent can implement and CI can run it without live AWS credentials.
findings.finding: AC3 asserts that 'a test asserts… the materialize script renders the guard value from its SSM parameter,' but the plan gives no description of how this is verified in-session: the script makes a live SSM call, so a naive test either requires a live AWS account or a mock/stub. Neither approach is specified, leaving the proving mechanism underspecified and potentially unreachable without a live account that may not be available in CI.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC3: 'a test asserts… the materialize script (per the `infra/gerrit/materialize-*.sh` precedent) renders the guard value from its SSM parameter'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit back-out / recovery procedure for the key-seeding step: what to do if the private key is lost after SSM seeding (re-generate + re-pin protocol), and what to do if `terraform apply` partially fails (e.g., `terraform destroy` scope or targeted destroy of the new resources).
findings.finding: The key-seeding step is explicitly acknowledged as irreversible ('the key exists nowhere else') but the plan provides no back-out procedure if seeding fails, the key is subsequently lost from SSM, or a partial `terraform apply` must be rolled back.
findings.location: What – Deploy + prove section; AC5
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Recovery / rollback' note to the Deploy+prove section: (1) If SSM seeding succeeds but the local key file is lost before commit, destroy the SSM param, regenerate the key, and re-seed before pinning. (2) If `terraform apply` partially fails, run `terraform apply` again (idempotent) or `terraform destroy -target=<new resources only>` to clean up before retrying. (3) If the deployed key is later compromised, remove the env entry from `trusted_environments.yaml`, delete/re-create the SSM param, and re-pin under a new `env_id`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Commit to a single script location in both the What section and AC3 (preferably `infra/gerrit/materialize-opcert-guard.sh` to match the established `materialize-*.sh` precedent), and update AC3 to assert the script exists at that path.
findings.finding: The materialize script location is left as 'infra/gerrit/ or infra/scripts/' in the What section, but AC3 does not pin the location — the AC will pass regardless of where the script is placed, leaving the canonical path undefined.
findings.location: What – Compose section; AC3
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'new script under infra/gerrit/ or infra/scripts/' with 'new script `infra/gerrit/materialize-opcert-guard.sh`' and add to AC3: 'the materialize script exists at `infra/gerrit/materialize-opcert-guard.sh`'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an ADR (or extend ADR 0046/0047, or add a section to CLAUDE.md / `infra/README.md`) that documents the two-layer auth model (SigV4 outer + origin-guard inner), its security rationale, and the guard-value rotation procedure (SSM update → materialize script → nginx reload).
findings.finding: The plan introduces a novel two-layer auth architecture (SigV4 outer perimeter + origin-guard inner header) as a security contract, but calls for no ADR or design-doc update to capture this decision, its security rationale, and its rotation procedure — making it undiscoverable to future operators and reviewers.
findings.location: What section (origin-guard provisioning path); Acceptance Criteria AC-1 and AC-3
findings.suggested_fix: Create `infra/docs/adr-0048-opcert-auth-layers.md` (or the project's ADR numbering convention) covering: (1) why origin-guard is needed even with SigV4 (defense-in-depth against direct-to-origin bypass), (2) how the guard value is provisioned and rotated, (3) the materialize script's role as the operationally critical link. Reference this ADR from the Terraform and nginx config files via comments.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit nginx rollback procedure to the deploy sequence: validate config with `nginx -t` before reload, keep a backup of the prior config, and document the revert command — especially because the shared box also serves Gerrit.
findings.finding: The plan specifies no rollback procedure for the nginx configuration change on the shared rebar-gerrit box. A malformed nginx config include (e.g., bad guard-header directive) would cause nginx reload to fail, taking down Gerrit and all existing services — with no stated path to quickly revert to the pre-change config.
findings.location: What — Compose/nginx wiring; Acceptance Criteria AC3 (nginx route with guard header)
findings.suggested_fix: In the deploy sequence, add: (1) `nginx -t` dry-run before reloading; (2) `cp nginx.conf nginx.conf.bak` before applying the new include; (3) a rollback step: `cp nginx.conf.bak nginx.conf && nginx -s reload`. Reference this in AC3.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the guard-value update lifecycle: document that re-materializing nginx (re-running the materialize script) is required whenever Terraform regenerates `random_password`, add an explicit 'guard value changed' detection step to the deploy runbook, and add observability (a logged/alerted 4xx spike or guard-mismatch counter on the opcert nginx route) so mismatches are not silent.
findings.finding: The `opcert-origin-guard` value is generated by Terraform's `random_password` and materialized into nginx by a script at deploy time, but the plan specifies no coordination procedure for the case where Terraform regenerates the value (e.g., state import, resource recreation, or explicit `taint`). During the window between Terraform re-apply and the next materialize-script run, API Gateway injects the new guard header while nginx still rejects it with the old expected value — causing silent 100% rejection of all opcert calls with no alerting.
findings.location: What — Terraform section (random_password + SSM origin-guard); What — materialize script; AC1

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a key retirement/rotation procedure to the plan (or scope it out explicitly with a reference to a future story): specify how to rotate the Ed25519 key in SSM and update the `trusted_environments.yaml` entry, and how to remove a decommissioned environment's entry from the trust store.
findings.finding: The plan specifies no retirement or rotation path for the Ed25519 private key stored in SSM (`/rebar/prod/opcert-ed25519-key`) nor for the corresponding public key pinned in `.rebar/trusted_environments.yaml`. These are long-lived shared secrets/state with no defined ownership for rotation, no expiry, and no cleanup procedure if the environment is decommissioned.
findings.location: What — SSM Parameter Store SecureString `/rebar/prod/opcert-ed25519-key`; AC4 (trusted_environments.yaml pinning)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Justify why `random_password`/`hashicorp/random` is preferred over the established `data "aws_ssm_parameter"` + operator CLI seeding pattern (used for all 12 existing secrets and already proven for the dual-use case in monitoring.tf), or switch to that pattern to avoid a new provider dependency with a single call-site.
findings.finding: The plan introduces `hashicorp/random` (a new provider) and `random_password` solely for the origin-guard value — a single use-site — while the codebase's established pattern for all 12 SSM secrets (ssm.tf `rebar_secret_params`) is CHANGEME placeholder + `lifecycle { ignore_changes = [value] }` + operator CLI seeding. The monitoring.tf already demonstrates an alternative that closes the dual-use loop without a new provider: `data "aws_ssm_parameter"` reads a value Terraform must reference (the alert-endpoint is read and injected into an SNS subscription). Applying the same approach here — operator seeds the origin-guard via `aws ssm put-parameter`, Terraform reads it as a `data "aws_ssm_parameter"` and maps it into the API GW integration — avoids the new provider and is consistent with all precedents. The plan does not justify why `random_password` is preferred over this established pattern.
findings.location: What section — 'the origin-guard header provisioning path: Terraform generates the guard value with `random_password`... the `hashicorp/random` provider is ADDED to `infra/terraform/versions.tf` `required_providers`'
findings.suggested_fix: Remove the `hashicorp/random` provider addition. Instead: add `/rebar/prod/opcert-origin-guard` to the existing `rebar_secret_params` list in ssm.tf (CHANGEME + lifecycle ignore_changes), have the operator seed it via `aws ssm put-parameter --type SecureString`, and reference it in the API GW integration via `data "aws_ssm_parameter" "opcert_origin_guard" { name = "/rebar/prod/opcert-origin-guard"; with_decryption = true }` — the same pattern monitoring.tf already uses for the SNS endpoint injection.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define or cite the existing schema/location for `.rebar/trusted_environments.yaml` and the 'era protocol' (field names, format, `added_at_commit` semantics) — either link to the document that specifies it or add a schema spec to this story's What/AC sections, since neither the file nor its defining document currently exists in the repo.
findings.finding: The plan asserts '.rebar/trusted_environments.yaml' already exists (or that the 'era protocol' defining its schema is already established) and specifies pinning the deployed environment's public key and env_id into it with an 'added_at_commit' field. No such file exists anywhere in the repo, and no schema, format, or era-protocol definition is discoverable — making AC4 rest on an unverifiable/nonexistent artifact.
findings.location: What section ('pin the environment's public key + env_id into .rebar/trusted_environments.yaml per the epic's era protocol'); AC4 ('The deployed environment's public key and env_id are pinned in .rebar/trusted_environments.yaml (with added_at_commit per the epic's era protocol)')

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the plan's nginx guard-injection description to acknowledge that the `include`-based nginx conf fragment mechanism is NEW work (no existing include pattern in `infra/nginx/rebar.conf.template`), and specify the concrete approach: e.g., add an `include /etc/nginx/conf.d/opcert-guard.conf;` directive to the template, and have the materialize script write that fragment file before nginx reload.
findings.finding: The plan asserts the guard value is materialized 'into the nginx conf include' following the stack's existing secret-materialization precedent, implying an nginx `include` mechanism already exists. In fact, the current infra/nginx/rebar.conf.template has no `include` directive and no include-based dynamic injection pattern — the entire nginx config is a single envsubst-rendered template. The nginx conf include mechanism is new work, not existing precedent.
findings.location: What section — Compose bullet: 'a materialize script...renders the guard value from its SSM parameter into the nginx conf include + service env by a materialize script following the stack's existing secret-materialization precedent'
findings.suggested_fix: In the What/Compose bullet, replace 'following the stack's existing secret-materialization precedent (infra/gerrit/materialize-*.sh)' with an explicit description: 'adds a new nginx include directive to rebar.conf.template (or a separate conf.d fragment) and writes the guard value into it before nginx reload — this include mechanism is new, distinct from the existing SSM-to-file precedent for container secrets.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `infra/scripts/compose-up.sh` to the edit-target list and specify the call site for the new materialize-opcert-guard.sh script (parallel to the materialize-g2p-config.sh call at line 177).
findings.finding: infra/scripts/compose-up.sh is an unenumerated consumer: every existing materialize script (materialize-deploy-key.sh, materialize-g2p-config.sh) is called from compose-up.sh before `docker compose up`, but the plan never names compose-up.sh as a file requiring modification to invoke the new opcert guard materialize script.
findings.location: What section — Compose/nginx wiring; infra/scripts/compose-up.sh lines 172-179
findings.suggested_fix: In the 'What' or AC section, name compose-up.sh as a required edit and specify that a call to the new materialize script (e.g. `bash "${REPO_ROOT}/infra/gerrit/materialize-opcert-guard.sh"` or whichever path is chosen) must be added before `docker compose up`, following the non-fatal pattern used for materialize-g2p-config.sh or the fail-closed pattern used for materialize-deploy-key.sh.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name `infra/nginx/rebar.conf.template` (and/or its rendering pipeline in the boot scripts) as an explicit edit target for the guarded `/opcert/` route, and specify whether the guard value is injected via envsubst extension, a separate nginx `include` snippet, or another mechanism.
findings.finding: infra/nginx/rebar.conf.template is the concrete nginx config file that must gain the guarded /opcert/ route, but the plan never names it as an explicit edit target — it says only 'nginx config' generically.
findings.location: What section — Compose/nginx wiring; infra/nginx/rebar.conf.template

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate `infra/scripts/autodeploy.sh` as a required edit target (to add opcert paths to BOT_PATHS / a parallel BOT_SERVICE trigger) OR explicitly scope opcert auto-deploy out as a manual-only operation in the plan.
findings.finding: infra/scripts/autodeploy.sh is an unenumerated consumer: its BOT_PATHS and BOT_SERVICE variables are hardcoded to the review-bot service only; adding a new opcert compose service means changes to Dockerfile.opcert (post-landing, during maintenance) will never trigger an auto-redeploy of the opcert service.
findings.location: What section — Compose service; infra/scripts/autodeploy.sh lines 54-55

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify explicitly that the new materialize script must (a) write the guard value into the nginx conf include AND (b) trigger `nginx -t && systemctl reload nginx`, and that `compose-up.sh` is extended to call this script (with fail-closed behavior matching the other materialize scripts) before bringing up the `opcert` container — so the guard is always enforced on both boot and re-deploy.
findings.finding: The plan's origin-guard materialize step silently assumes a host nginx reload, but the existing materialize-script precedent only writes files to bind-mounted container dirs — it never reloads a host service. nginx is a host package (not a container), so writing the guard value into a nginx conf include also requires `nginx -t && systemctl reload nginx` on the host. The plan names the precedent but does not specify (a) where in the boot sequence the new materialize script is called relative to nginx reload, (b) whether `compose-up.sh` is extended to invoke it and trigger the reload, or (c) what happens when Terraform is re-applied and the guard value changes: SSM is updated but nginx continues serving the old guard until the next manual re-materialize + reload.
findings.location: What section — Compose/nginx wiring paragraph; 'a materialize script following the stack's existing secret-materialization precedent (infra/gerrit/materialize-*.sh — SSM → file, same pattern)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `materialize-opcert-guard.sh` script (under `infra/gerrit/` or `infra/scripts/`) that: (1) fetches `/rebar/prod/opcert-origin-guard` from SSM, (2) renders the nginx conf include (e.g. `add_header`-style check or a `map`/`if` block), (3) runs `nginx -t && systemctl reload nginx`. Call it from `compose-up.sh` as a FAIL-CLOSED step (same pattern as `materialize-g2p-config.sh`) before `docker compose up`. Document in the plan that guard rotation requires re-running this script.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Align leaf AC4 (and the 'Deploy + prove' section) to use `added_at_log_position` (stamped by `rebar trusted-env`, a tickets-branch log position) as the era-boundary field, replacing the superseded `added_at_commit` (explicit git SHA on `main`), to match the parent epic's 'Authoritative era anchor' schema.
findings.finding: The leaf specifies `added_at_commit` as the era-boundary field when pinning the environment's public key in `.rebar/trusted_environments.yaml`, but the parent epic's 'Authoritative era anchor' section (which explicitly supersedes the earlier 'Trust config & era-validity protocol') redefines era boundaries as `added_at_log_position`/`revoked_at_log_position` (tickets-branch log positions stamped by the `rebar trusted-env` helper). The leaf names the old, superseded field — a direct contradiction of the parent's current deliverable definition.
findings.location: Leaf 'Deploy + prove' section and AC4: '(with `added_at_commit` per the epic's era protocol)'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace '(with `added_at_commit` per the epic's era protocol)' in both the 'Deploy + prove' section and AC4 with '(with `added_at_log_position` stamped by `rebar trusted-env` at the current tickets-branch tip, per the epic's era-at-storage-anchor protocol)'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify and explicitly cite the exact `aws_apigatewayv2_integration` `request_parameters` attribute key format required to inject a static header (e.g., `"append:header.X-Opcert-Guard"`) and add it as a named specification in the IaC AC or a linked implementation note, so the executor has an unambiguous target.
findings.finding: The plan asserts Terraform can inject the origin-guard value as a static `X-Opcert-Guard` request header on the `aws_apigatewayv2_integration` resource, but does not cite or verify the exact resource attribute and key format required (`request_parameters` with the `append:header.X-Opcert-Guard` key). The Terraform AWS provider's HTTP API integration `request_parameters` attribute uses a non-obvious key syntax; a wrong key silently produces a valid `terraform apply` while never injecting the header — breaking the inner perimeter with no deploy-time signal.
findings.location: What section — 'an API Gateway HTTP API … injecting a shared origin-guard header … maps it as a static request-header parameter (`X-Opcert-Guard`) on the API Gateway integration'
findings.suggested_fix: In the AC for `infra/terraform/` (AC line 1), add: 'the integration's `request_parameters` block uses key `"append:header.X-Opcert-Guard"` (the `aws_apigatewayv2_integration` `request_parameters` attribute syntax for static header injection on HTTP API integrations).' This pins the capability assertion to the specific, verifiable attribute format.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add observability for the new API Gateway HTTP API: specify a CloudWatch log group for API GW access logs (access_log_settings), enable CloudWatch execution logging or metrics, and add at least one CloudWatch alarm (e.g., 5XXError rate or IntegrationLatency) wired to the existing SNS alert topic.
findings.finding: The plan specifies no logging, metrics, or alarms for the new API Gateway HTTP API endpoint — no access log destination, no CloudWatch execution logging, and no alarm on 4xx/5xx error rates or latency for the opcert service.
findings.location: What section — API Gateway HTTP API declaration; AC line 1 (infra/terraform/ declarations)
findings.suggested_fix: In the new Terraform file for the API GW, add: (1) an aws_cloudwatch_log_group for access logs, (2) access_log_settings on the aws_apigatewayv2_stage pointing to that log group, (3) an aws_cloudwatch_metric_alarm on 5XXError ≥ 1 for 2 evaluation periods wired to aws_sns_topic.alerts — matching the pattern in monitoring.tf.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Address the random_password drift scenario: either (a) add lifecycle { ignore_changes = [result] } to the random_password resource so the guard never regenerates after initial creation, or (b) document in the plan/runbook that any guard rotation requires an immediate re-materialization on the VM and a re-deploy of the compose service.
findings.finding: The plan does not address the drift scenario where Terraform regenerates the random_password origin-guard value (e.g., after state loss, a targeted taint, or state import without the random resource) while the nginx config on the VM still holds the previous value — silently breaking the origin-guard loop until the materialize script is manually re-run.
findings.location: What section — 'origin-guard header provisioning path'; materialize script description
findings.suggested_fix: Add lifecycle { ignore_changes = [result] } to the aws_random_password.origin_guard resource so the value is generated once and never changed by a subsequent apply, matching the ignore_changes = [value] pattern used on the SSM SecureString parameters for other secrets.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in the What section that HTTP API AWS_IAM auth is declared via `authorization_type = "AWS_IAM"` on each `aws_apigatewayv2_route` resource (NOT via a separate `aws_apigatewayv2_authorizer` resource, which HTTP API does not support for the AWS_IAM type).
findings.finding: HTTP API (APIGatewayV2) AWS_IAM authorization is configured per-route via `authorization_type = "AWS_IAM"` on `aws_apigatewayv2_route` — there is NO separate `aws_apigatewayv2_authorizer` resource for the AWS_IAM type. The plan's phrase 'an IAM (SigV4) authorizer' and 'IAM (SigV4) authorizer' echoes the REST API (v1) concept of a named authorizer resource; if an implementer follows that mental model and creates an `aws_apigatewayv2_authorizer` with `authorizer_type = "AWS_IAM"`, Terraform will reject it (HTTP API authorizers support only JWT and REQUEST/LAMBDA types). This is a capability-surface mismatch that would cause a terraform plan/apply failure.
findings.location: What section — 'an API Gateway HTTP API with an IAM (SigV4) authorizer'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'an IAM (SigV4) authorizer' with: 'each route declares `authorization_type = "AWS_IAM"` (the aws_apigatewayv2_route attribute — HTTP API does not support a separate IAM authorizer resource; JWT and REQUEST/LAMBDA are the only supported aws_apigatewayv2_authorizer types for HTTP API).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add the exact `request_parameters` syntax for the static `X-Opcert-Guard` header injection in the `aws_apigatewayv2_integration` block to the AC or What section (e.g., `"overwrite:header.X-Opcert-Guard" = "<guard_value>"`) so the implementer does not have to infer the APIGatewayV2 parameter-mapping notation.
findings.finding: The plan specifies that Terraform maps the origin-guard value as a 'static request-header parameter (`X-Opcert-Guard`) on the API Gateway integration' but does not state the required `request_parameters` syntax for `aws_apigatewayv2_integration`. The correct form is `overwrite:header.X-Opcert-Guard = "<value>"` inside `request_parameters` — this uses a specific Terraform/APIGateway parameter-mapping syntax that differs from a simple key=value map and is not self-evident from the plan. An incorrect syntax silently produces an integration that omits the header, breaking the origin-guard check without a terraform error.
findings.location: What section — 'injecting a shared origin-guard header' / 'maps it as a static request-header parameter (`X-Opcert-Guard`) on the API Gateway integration'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Declare the API Gateway → EC2 origin integration URI scheme explicitly: either assert the integration URI is `https://` (box nginx serves TLS on port 443 per the existing security group) and add an AC asserting this, or acknowledge the HTTP/public-internet origin leg and document its accepted risk.
findings.finding: The protocol used for the API Gateway → EC2 origin integration leg is undeclared. The plan rejects VPC Link, meaning the integration reaches the box over the public internet. The phrase 'HTTP-integrated to the box's nginx origin' is ambiguous: 'HTTP' could mean the AWS HTTP_PROXY integration type (which supports both https:// and http:// backend URIs) or plaintext HTTP. 'Over TLS' in the same sentence qualifies the client-facing invocation path. If the backend integration URI is http://, the X-Opcert-Guard header value and any certificate material in responses traverse the public internet unencrypted after API GW strips SigV4, defeating the guard's confidentiality and exposing the service's output to interception.
findings.location: What section — 'an API Gateway HTTP API … invoke permission over TLS, HTTP-integrated to the box's nginx origin'
findings.suggested_fix: Set the API Gateway integration URI to `https://<box-hostname>/opcert/` (port 443, which is already open on the security group and served by nginx with a Let's Encrypt cert per the existing setup). Add to the AC: 'the API Gateway integration URI uses HTTPS (port 443)'. This requires no new resources and closes the transit gap without VPC Link.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Generate the origin-guard value out-of-band (e.g., `openssl rand -hex 32`) and seed it into SSM via `aws ssm put-parameter` (same operator procedure as the ed25519 key), then declare the SSM parameter with `value = "CHANGEME"` + `lifecycle { ignore_changes = [value] }` — keeping the authoritative secret out of Terraform state consistent with all 12 existing secret parameters.
findings.finding: The origin-guard value is generated by Terraform's `random_password` resource and will be stored as a plaintext attribute in the Terraform state file. All 12 existing secret parameters follow the established pattern of 'CHANGEME placeholder + operator-seeded out-of-band + `ignore_changes = [value]`' specifically to keep secret material out of Terraform state. The plan breaks this pattern for origin-guard without acknowledging it: anyone (or any CI role) with `s3:GetObject` on the state bucket — including the existing `rebar-terraform-plan` GitHub Actions OIDC role, which holds `ReadOnlyAccess` — can extract the guard value from state and use it to bypass API Gateway by hitting nginx port 80 directly.
findings.location: What section — 'Terraform generates the guard value with `random_password` … stores it as SSM SecureString `/rebar/prod/opcert-origin-guard`'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the `random_password` + direct SSM value approach with the existing pattern: declare `aws_ssm_parameter` for `/rebar/prod/opcert-origin-guard` with `value = "CHANGEME"` and `lifecycle { ignore_changes = [value] }`. Add to the 'Deploy + prove' section: generate the guard value with `openssl rand -hex 32` and seed it with `aws ssm put-parameter --name /rebar/prod/opcert-origin-guard --value <value> --type SecureString --overwrite` before `terraform apply` (or on first apply after the placeholder is created). Remove the `hashicorp/random` provider addition (it was introduced solely for this use case).

findings.finding: The plan's key security constraint — '`execute-api:Invoke` granted only to the named admin role (identity policy)' — references a 'named admin role' that does not exist anywhere in infra/terraform/*.tf. No admin role or IAM principal is defined, named, or even described by ARN pattern in the current IaC. Since the plan relies on this role as the SOLE caller restriction mechanism (HTTP API has no resource policy), and the role is presented as something that will be granted `execute-api:Invoke`, it is unclear whether this refers to: (a) one of the existing roles already in iam.tf (none of which are 'admin' roles), (b) the GitHub Actions OIDC role `rebar-terraform-plan`, (c) a role that lives outside this repo's IaC, or (d) a new role to be created. The plan never names or identifies this role. An unresolvable identity for the sole caller-restriction principal is a security-critical gap.
findings.location: What section — 'execute-api:Invoke on this API is granted ONLY via a policy attached to the named admin role'; AC#1 — 'execute-api:Invoke granted only to the named admin role (identity policy; a test asserts no other principal is granted)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence in the What section naming the exact IAM role (e.g., 'the operator's IAM role, whose ARN is stored in a Terraform variable `var.admin_role_arn`' or 'a new `rebar-opcert-caller` role declared in this story's IaC') that will receive the `execute-api:Invoke` grant, and ensure AC#1 references it by the same name.

findings.finding: The plan grants execute-api:Invoke 'only to the named admin role' (What section, AC#1) but no such pre-existing admin IAM role is defined anywhere in infra/terraform/iam.tf or infra/terraform/iam_s2.tf / iam_s4a.tf. The only roles in the Terraform codebase are the EC2 instance role, the DLM lifecycle role, and the GHA OIDC terraform-plan role — none of which is an 'admin role' for human operator use. The plan does not name the role, state that it must be created, or reference an out-of-band role by ARN.
findings.location: What section ('execute-api:Invoke on this API is granted ONLY via a policy attached to the named admin role'); AC#1 ('execute-api:Invoke granted only to the named admin role')

findings.finding: The 'named admin role' that is allowed to invoke the API Gateway is referenced throughout (What section, AC#1) but is never identified — no role name, ARN pattern, or derivation rule is given. An executor cannot write the `aws_iam_policy` / `aws_api_gateway_v2_route` authorization scope or the test assertion without knowing which role to restrict to.
findings.location: What section (API Gateway bullet) and AC#1: 'restricted to a named admin role'
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence to the What section and AC#1, e.g.: 'invoke permission granted exclusively to the `rebar-admin` IAM role (already declared in `infra/terraform/iam.tf`); the test asserts the integration's authorization_type is AWS_IAM and the resource policy restricts `execute-api:Invoke` to that role ARN.'

findings.finding: The plan's key security constraint — 'execute-api:Invoke granted only to the named admin role' — references a role that is never identified by name or ARN anywhere in the plan, leaving the IaC implementation underspecified for its most security-critical requirement.
findings.location: What section (API Gateway IAM authorizer paragraph); AC line 1 ('execute-api:Invoke granted only to the named admin role (identity policy; a test asserts no other principal is granted)')
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence such as: 'The admin role is the existing `rebar-admin` role declared in `infra/terraform/iam.tf` (or looked up via `data.aws_iam_role.admin`); the policy attachment targets this role by ARN.' If it is an out-of-account role, provide the ARN as a Terraform variable with a documented default.

findings.finding: The plan's access-control model for `execute-api:Invoke` depends on granting the permission to a 'named admin role,' but this role is never named, identified, or declared anywhere — not in the plan body, not in any existing `infra/terraform/` file. No IAM role with admin/operator scope exists in the current IaC. Without a concrete principal (role name or ARN), `terraform apply` cannot produce the identity policy, and the stated security invariant ('no other principal/policy in the IaC grants it') cannot be verified.
findings.location: What section: 'execute-api:Invoke on this API is granted ONLY via a policy attached to the named admin role'; AC#1: 'execute-api:Invoke granted only to the named admin role (identity policy; a test asserts no other principal is granted)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the 'What / Terraform' bullet: 'The `execute-api:Invoke` identity policy is attached to `<role-name>` (e.g. the operator's AWS SSO permission-set role, referenced as a `data "aws_iam_role"` data source in the new HCL file). The role name is recorded as a Terraform variable `var.opcert_invoke_role_name` with a default matching the live account's operator role ARN.'

findings.finding: The plan's entire caller-restriction model depends on `execute-api:Invoke` being granted 'only to the named admin role (identity policy),' but the 'named admin role' is never identified — no role name, ARN, or derivation rule appears in the plan or existing IaC. Without a named principal, the IaC cannot implement the grant, the AC#1 assertion ('no other principal is granted') cannot be evaluated, and the SigV4-signed round-trip in AC#5 has no defined signing identity. This is an undeclared access posture on a public boundary-crossing path.
findings.location: What section ('execute-api:Invoke on this API is granted ONLY via a policy attached to the named admin role'); AC#1 ('execute-api:Invoke granted only to the named admin role … a test asserts no other principal is granted')
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence to the What section: 'The identity policy attaching `execute-api:Invoke` is added to the existing `rebar-terraform-plan` role (or a new `rebar-opcert-caller` role declared in `infra/terraform/iam_opcert.tf`); the role name/ARN is referenced by a `data.aws_iam_role` in the new opcert Terraform file.' Update AC#1 to name the role explicitly.

findings.finding: The leaf's AC4 pins the deployed environment's public key in `.rebar/trusted_environments.yaml` using the field name `added_at_commit`, citing 'the epic's era protocol.' But the parent epic's later 'Authoritative era anchor (security finding #5)' section explicitly SUPERSEDES the earlier 'Trust config & era-validity protocol' section, changing era boundary fields from `added_at_commit`/`revoked_at_commit` (explicit main SHAs) to `added_at_log_position`/`revoked_at_log_position` (operator-stamped tickets-branch log positions). The leaf's AC4 references the superseded field name, contradicting the parent's final, authoritative era schema.
findings.location: Leaf AC4 ('with `added_at_commit` per the epic's era protocol') vs. parent epic 'Authoritative era anchor (security finding #5)' section
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the AC4 parenthetical from '(with `added_at_commit` per the epic's era protocol)' to '(with `added_at_log_position` stamped by `rebar trusted-env` at the current tickets tip, per the epic's era-at-storage-anchor protocol — the superseding design in the "Authoritative era anchor" section)'.

findings.finding: The leaf's AC#4 references `added_at_commit` as the era-protocol field when pinning the deployed environment's key in `.rebar/trusted_environments.yaml`, but the parent's 'Authoritative era anchor' section explicitly supersedes the earlier 'Trust config & era-validity protocol' section and replaces `added_at_commit`/`revoked_at_commit` (explicit SHAs on main) with `added_at_log_position`/`revoked_at_log_position` (tickets-branch log positions stamped by a `rebar trusted-env` helper). The parent wins; the leaf diverges from the parent's authoritative, superseding field specification.
findings.location: Leaf AC#4: 'with `added_at_commit` per the epic's era protocol'
findings.suggested_fix: In AC#4, replace '(with `added_at_commit` per the epic's era protocol)' with '(with `added_at_log_position` stamped by the `rebar trusted-env` helper per the epic's superseding "Authoritative era anchor" protocol)'.

findings.finding: The leaf's AC4 and plan text pin the deployed environment key in `.rebar/trusted_environments.yaml` using `added_at_commit` (an explicit git SHA on main), which is the superseded era schema. The parent's authoritative final design resolution (security finding #5, Option B era-at-storage-anchor, the last substantive comment on the epic) replaced this with `added_at_log_position`/`revoked_at_log_position` (tickets-branch log positions operator-stamped via `rebar trusted-env` helper). The leaf diverges from the parent by implementing the old schema that the parent explicitly superseded.
findings.location: Leaf AC4: 'with `added_at_commit` per the epic's era protocol'; leaf What section: 'pin the environment's public key + `env_id` into `.rebar/trusted_environments.yaml` per the epic's era protocol'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'with `added_at_commit` per the epic's era protocol' in AC4 with 'with `added_at_log_position` (the current tickets-branch tip, stamped by `rebar trusted-env add`) per the parent epic's era-at-storage-anchor resolution (Option B, security finding #5)'. Update the 'Deploy + prove' narrative step to call `rebar trusted-env add` to stamp the log position rather than manually recording a main-branch SHA.

findings.finding: The leaf's `file_impact` metadata declares `path: 'infra/opcert/'` with reason `'Terraform for API GW + Fargate + Secrets Manager'` — both the directory path and the reason are stale artifacts from a pre-cost-verification design that the parent epic explicitly rejected. The parent's settled design targets `infra/terraform/` (not a new `infra/opcert/` module) and explicitly rejects Fargate and Secrets Manager; the leaf's own plan body is internally consistent with the parent, but the ticket-level `file_impact` field contradicts both.
findings.location: Leaf ticket `file_impact`: `[{"path": "infra/opcert/", "reason": "Terraform for API GW + Fargate + Secrets Manager"}]`
findings.suggested_fix: Set file_impact to: [{"path": "infra/terraform/", "reason": "API Gateway HTTP API + IAM SigV4 authorizer + two SSM SecureString parameters"}, {"path": "infra/compose/", "reason": "opcert service entry + guarded nginx route + materialize script"}, {"path": ".rebar/trusted_environments.yaml", "reason": "pin deployed env public key + env_id"}]

findings.finding: The plan asserts that caller access to the HTTP API is controlled exclusively via identity policy ('execute-api:Invoke on this API is granted ONLY via a policy attached to the named admin role — no other principal/policy in the IaC grants it'), and explicitly states 'an HTTP API has no resource policy' — but AWS API Gateway HTTP APIs (v2) DO support resource policies. The plan never resolves whether a resource policy will be added as belt-and-suspenders, and the identity-policy-only claim cannot guarantee exclusivity beyond the IaC boundary (any existing AdministratorAccess-bearing identity in the account already has invoke rights). This is an unverified capability assertion: the security guarantee as stated cannot be upheld by identity-policy-only IaC alone.
findings.location: What section — 'Terraform — extend the existing infra/terraform/' bullet; AC#1
findings.suggested_fix: Add an `aws_apigatewayv2_api_mapping` resource-policy statement (or an `aws_api_gateway_resource_policy` equivalent for v2 via the `aws_apigatewayv2_api` `body` or a separate `aws_apigatewayv2_authorizer`) that explicitly denies `execute-api:Invoke` to `*` and allows only the named admin role ARN. This closes the gap that identity-policy-only enforcement cannot cover for pre-existing broad-grant principals in the account.

findings.finding: The plan specifies that the new materialize script writes the origin-guard value into 'an nginx conf include', but nginx is a host service (not a container). After the materialize script writes the guard value to the nginx include file, nginx must be reloaded (`nginx -t && systemctl reload nginx`) before the new guard value takes effect. The plan does not specify that the script triggers an nginx reload. The existing materialize precedents (`materialize-deploy-key.sh`, `materialize-g2p-config.sh`) write to bind-mounted container directories and do NOT reload nginx — making this a new behavior that must be explicitly specified.
findings.location: What section — 'a materialize script … SSM → file, same pattern, new script under `infra/gerrit/` or `infra/scripts/`'; Acceptance Criteria AC#3 — 'the materialize script … renders the guard value from its SSM parameter'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
